mappings.rationale: Directly covers U.S. history including the period 1763-1920 encompassing the Revolution, Civil War, westward expansion, and industrialization discussed in this section.
Directly addresses the American Revolutionary period (1763-87), including opposition to British taxation, the Declaration of Independence, and the War of Independence covered in subsection A.1.a.
Covers the Constitutional Convention, federal Constitution, and the struggle for ratification (1787-89) discussed in subsection A.1.b, as well as the formation of early national policies.
Directly relevant to Lincoln's election (1860), the secession crisis, the Civil War, and emancipation policies discussed in subsections A.2-3.
Directly covers the Civil War (1860-65), including sectionalism, secession, military campaigns, and emancipation discussed in subsection A.3.
Covers the institution of slavery, the Abolitionist movement, and the economic and psychological bases of slavery central to subsections A.2 and A.3.
Directly addresses the Abolitionist movement discussed in subsections A.2.c and A.3.a as a major reform activity and driver of sectional conflict.
Directly covers Radical Reconstruction (1866-77) and the New South (1877-1900), including freedmen, sharecropping, and Jim Crow legislation in subsection B.1.
Relevant to the many presidential administrations discussed from Washington through Wilson, including their policies on expansion, reform, and war.
Relevant to the section's discussion of congressional politics including Radical Republican Congress, legislative supremacy period (1877-1900), and tariff legislation.
Relevant to the role of the Supreme Court under Marshall discussed in subsection A.2.a and the general development of federal judicial power.
Covers the federal Constitution and its ratification (1787-89) discussed in subsection A.1.b, as well as constitutional development through amendments during Reconstruction.
Relevant to the formation of political parties from Federalists and Democratic-Republicans through the emergence of the Republican Party discussed throughout subsections A and B.
Covers the broad political history of the United States from its founding through the Progressive Era, directly relevant to the entire section.
Relevant to the development of federal law, civil rights legislation, and the Sherman Anti-Trust Act discussed in subsections B.1 and B.2.f.
Tocqueville's analysis of American democracy is directly relevant to Jacksonian democracy (subsection A.2.b) and the extension of the franchise.
Relevant to the Indian removal policy under Jackson (subsection A.2.b), violation of Indian treaties, and Indian wars in subsection B.2.b.iv.
Directly covers the development of the West (1865-1900) including mining, cattle industry, railroads, and Indian conflicts discussed in subsection B.2.b.
Relevant to the section's extensive coverage of Southern history including slavery, the Civil War, Reconstruction, and the New South with Jim Crow legislation.
Relevant to the discussion of German and Irish immigration (1830-50) and southern/eastern European immigration in subsections A.2.a.iii and B.2.a.
Relevant to social reform movements, the 'Age of Reform' (1830-50), and Progressive Era reforms discussed in subsections A.2.c and B.3.b.
Relevant to the religious dimensions of slavery, the Abolitionist movement, and the Black experience during Reconstruction discussed in subsections A.2-3 and B.1.
Directly relevant to the section's extensive coverage of slavery, the Civil War, Reconstruction, freedmen, Jim Crow, and the black response to white supremacy.
Relevant to the cultural developments discussed in subsections A.2.e and B.4, including literature, poetry, the Transcendentalist movement, and the arts.
Relevant to the Transcendentalist movement, growth of American philosophy, and intellectual developments discussed in subsections A.2.e and B.4.
Provides background context for the colonial period leading up to the Revolutionary era that opens this section in 1763.
Directly covers the Progressive Movement (c. 1896-1920) including muckrakers, social reform, and the administrations of Roosevelt, Taft, and Wilson discussed in subsection B.3.b.
Relevant to U.S. entry into World War I, mobilization, the Western Front campaigns, and Wilson's role at the Paris Peace Conference discussed in subsection B.3.c.
Relevant to the War of 1812 context and Madison's administration discussed in subsection A.1.b.iii, as well as background to the Monroe Doctrine.
Relevant to Jacksonian democracy, extension of the franchise, and the development of democratic institutions discussed throughout the section.
Directly relevant to the establishment of the federal system, the Constitutional Convention, and the ongoing tension between federal and state power discussed throughout the section.
Relevant to the industrialization of the American economy, development of trusts and holding companies, and the manufacturing boom discussed in subsection B.2.c.
Relevant to the industrialization and manufacturing transformation of the U.S. economy discussed in subsections A.2 and B.2.c.
Relevant to U.S. emergence as an imperial power, the Spanish-American War, and acquisition of overseas territories discussed in subsection B.3.a.
Directly covers Canadian history including British colonial rule, Confederation (1867), and development through 1920 discussed in subsection C.
Relevant to naval aspects of the War of Independence, the War of 1812, the Civil War naval campaigns, and the Spanish-American War discussed throughout the section.
Covers military aspects of the Revolution, War of 1812, Mexican War, Civil War, Indian wars, and Spanish-American War discussed throughout the section.
Relevant to the development of trusts, holding companies, railroads, and the industrialization of the economy discussed in subsection B.2.c-d.
Relevant to the context of U.S.-Mexican relations, the Mexican War (1846-48), and intervention in Mexican affairs under Wilson discussed in subsections A.2.d and B.3.b.iii.
Relevant to the growth of American nationalism, the Era of Good Feelings, and sectionalism discussed in subsection A.2.a.
Relevant to the waves of immigration from Europe, the Irish and German immigration (1830-50), and southern/eastern European immigration discussed in subsections A.2.a.iii and B.2.a.
Covers U.S. foreign relations from the early republic through World War I, including the Monroe Doctrine, Open Door policy, and Roosevelt Corollary discussed throughout the section.
Relevant to the discussion of racial dimensions of slavery, Jim Crow legislation, white supremacy, and prejudice against immigrants discussed in subsections A.3, B.1, and B.2.a.
Directly relevant to Wilson's policies at the Paris Peace Conference and the U.S. Senate rejection of the Treaty discussed in subsection B.3.c.iii.
Relevant to U.S. diplomatic history including the Open Door policy, Roosevelt Corollary, and Wilson's peace diplomacy discussed in subsections B.3.a and B.3.c.
Relevant to the cultural development of American poetry discussed in subsections A.2.e and B.4.a.
The temperance movement was a major reform activity in the Age of Reform (1830-50) and the Progressive Era discussed in subsections A.2.c and B.3.b.
Provides context for understanding the post-Civil War civil rights struggles, Black Codes, and federal civil rights legislation during Reconstruction discussed in subsection B.1.
Relevant to the history of Asian immigration and the anti-Chinese sentiment reflected in westward expansion and immigration policies discussed in subsection B.2.
Hamilton was a key architect of the US Constitution and federal government, directly relevant to the section's coverage of American political development from 1763 to 1920.

mappings.vsiAuthor: Daina Ramey Berry, Kali N. Gross
mappings.rationale: Covers the history and aftermath of slavery in America including abolition, Black Codes, and Jim Crow, directly relevant to this section's treatment of US social and political development.
